bins.name: Unprecedented scale of load growth
bins.desc: Data center load requests are generating 5 to 10 percent annual growth projections in regions that had been flat for two decades, creating novel challenges for interconnection processes and cost allocation that existing frameworks were not designed to handle.
quotes: Hyperscale data centers are placing unprecedented strains on our electricity system and on traditional interconnection and cost allocation practices.
Regions that have not had meaningful load growth in two decades are now facing projections of 5-10% growth every year for the foreseeable future.

bins.name: Socialize costs to direct causers
bins.desc: ELPC argues that high-voltage connection facilities built solely to serve individual large-load customers are improperly placed into the shared transmission rate base, spreading costs worth billions of dollars annually to existing ratepayers. ELPC supports rules and state tariff approaches that assign those costs directly to the customers who cause them.
quotes: The consequences are higher prices for consumers and regulators at every level facing significant questions as to how to meet electricity demand growth while ensuring affordability and reliability.

large load customers often require new lines and substations at voltages of 138kV or higher. Mr. Jacobs explained that although such high-voltage connection facilities are essentially line extensions that the utility designs to serve a specific customer, the utilities socialize their costs by placing them into the transmission rate base.
Mr. Jacobs provided his analysis showing that such connection facilities added up to $4.1 billion across PJM in 2024 alone, with costs continuing to increase in 2025 and beyond.

the California Public Utilities Commission recently issued an interim order requiring new transmission-level customers seeking retail services to be responsible for the initial costs of all transmission facilities, rather than those costs being borne by ratepayers.
the Company practices that do provide tracking of these transmission costs are not used to pursue cost allocation that would apply cost-causation principles.

the Company makes decisions regarding functionalization of new facilities as transmission or distribution without any oversight. Such decisions have a major impact on retail rates. ComEd should make these decisions with greater transparency and oversight.
The Company does not plan to use Cluster Studies to assign the costs of new facilities to the Large Demand Project Applicants or Customers that cause those costs.

bins.name: Large loads must pay upgrade costs
bins.desc: ELPC endorses requiring large-load applicants to bear the incremental transmission upgrade and extension costs their connections require. Evidence from PJM shows $4.1 billion in socialized connection costs in 2024 alone, and state commissions in California and Kansas have already moved to direct cost assignment.
quotes: large load customers often require new lines and substations at voltages of 138kV or higher. Mr. Jacobs explained that although such high-voltage connection facilities are essentially line extensions that the utility designs to serve a specific customer, the utilities socialize their costs by placing them into the transmission rate base.
Mr. Jacobs provided his analysis showing that such connection facilities added up to $4.1 billion across PJM in 2024 alone, with costs continuing to increase in 2025 and beyond.

the California Public Utilities Commission recently issued an interim order requiring new transmission-level customers seeking retail services to be responsible for the initial costs of all transmission facilities, rather than those costs being borne by ratepayers.

the Commission should order ComEd to collect deposits for the incremental costs of Large Demand Project Applicants or Customers, regardless of whether those costs are deemed by ComEd to be transmission costs or distribution costs.

bins.name: State retail jurisdiction must be preserved
bins.desc: ELPC urges FERC to confine any rulemaking to its own transmission jurisdiction and to affirm explicitly that states retain authority to impose terms and conditions on retail service for large-load customers. A cooperative federalism framework would allow both levels of government to address the problem without one displacing the other.
quotes: We encourage the Commission to avoid interfering with the efforts of state commissions to protect retail customers and the grid from the impacts of large load additions.
ELPC encourages the Commission to follow a cooperative federalism model in its response to large load interconnections.
The Commission should make clear that states, as regulators of retail customers, can impose terms and conditions on retail service requests of large load customers.

These decisions reflect sound exercises of states' authority over retail customers. In the face of the reliability and ratepayer risks that large load interconnections create, state commissions have a responsibility under state law to preserve reliability and protect just and reasonable rates.
Any rule that this Commission considers must avoid interference with the states in their role as regulators of retail customers.

bins.name: Cooperative federalism model
bins.desc: ELPC frames the ideal FERC response as a cooperative federalism model: FERC addresses wholesale transmission rates and reliability, states address retail customer protection and tariff conditions. FERC should not issue rules that preempt the state proceedings already underway.
quotes: We encourage the Commission to avoid interfering with the efforts of state commissions to protect retail customers and the grid from the impacts of large load additions.
ELPC encourages the Commission to follow a cooperative federalism model in its response to large load interconnections.
The Commission should make clear that states, as regulators of retail customers, can impose terms and conditions on retail service requests of large load customers.

These decisions reflect sound exercises of states' authority over retail customers. In the face of the reliability and ratepayer risks that large load interconnections create, state commissions have a responsibility under state law to preserve reliability and protect just and reasonable rates.
Any rule that this Commission considers must avoid interference with the states in their role as regulators of retail customers.

bins.name: Protect existing ratepayers from cost shifts
bins.desc: Existing customers face billions in cost shifts because utilities functionalize large-load connection infrastructure as transmission and spread it across the rate base. State commissions and FERC should require deposits, direct cost assignment, and tariff provisions that prevent this subsidy from flowing from ordinary customers to data center operators.
quotes: The consequences are higher prices for consumers and regulators at every level facing significant questions as to how to meet electricity demand growth while ensuring affordability and reliability.

large load customers often require new lines and substations at voltages of 138kV or higher. Mr. Jacobs explained that although such high-voltage connection facilities are essentially line extensions that the utility designs to serve a specific customer, the utilities socialize their costs by placing them into the transmission rate base.

the California Public Utilities Commission recently issued an interim order requiring new transmission-level customers seeking retail services to be responsible for the initial costs of all transmission facilities, rather than those costs being borne by ratepayers.

the Commission should order ComEd to collect deposits for the incremental costs of Large Demand Project Applicants or Customers, regardless of whether those costs are deemed by ComEd to be transmission costs or distribution costs.

bins.name: Use studies to track and assign costs
bins.desc: Cluster studies already identify the transmission and distribution reinforcements triggered by individual large-load requests, but utilities do not use that information to assign costs back to the causers. ELPC recommends that study results be tied directly to cost allocation.
quotes: the Company practices that do provide tracking of these transmission costs are not used to pursue cost allocation that would apply cost-causation principles.
The Company does not plan to use Cluster Studies to assign the costs of new facilities to the Large Demand Project Applicants or Customers that cause those costs.

bins.name: Deposits must cover transmission costs
bins.desc: Current deposit proposals cover only distribution facilities; ELPC recommends requiring deposits for all incremental costs regardless of how the utility classifies the facilities, to prevent ratepayers from absorbing stranded transmission investments if a large-load project withdraws.
quotes: the Commission should order ComEd to collect deposits for the incremental costs of Large Demand Project Applicants or Customers, regardless of whether those costs are deemed by ComEd to be transmission costs or distribution costs.

bins.name: Transparent functionalization decisions
bins.desc: ComEd decides on a case-by-case basis whether to classify new facilities as distribution or transmission, with no external oversight. ELPC argues those decisions should be made with greater transparency and subject to regulatory review, as they determine which customers bear the resulting costs.
quotes: the Company makes decisions regarding functionalization of new facilities as transmission or distribution without any oversight. Such decisions have a major impact on retail rates. ComEd should make these decisions with greater transparency and oversight.

bins.name: PJM cost socialization scale
bins.desc: ELPC cites PJM as the region where the socialization of large-load connection costs is most documented, with $4.1 billion in such costs in 2024 alone and the trend worsening into 2025 and beyond.
quotes: Mr. Jacobs provided his analysis showing that such connection facilities added up to $4.1 billion across PJM in 2024 alone, with costs continuing to increase in 2025 and beyond.
